title: Fixed-Size Chunking
concept: Splitting text every N tokens or characters regardless of sentence or paragraph boundaries; simple but naive.

exercises.prompt: To help an LLM find information in a massive library of documents, what is the first step we must take with the raw text?

options.text: Compressing whole files into ZIP archives.
Breaking long texts into smaller, retrievable chunks.

exercises.explain: Before diving into advanced semantic search, we need to understand how we prepare long documents so that an LLM's retriever can find the right information efficiently.

cards.title: Chop It Up
cards.body: LLMs have a limit on how much text they can read at once. To feed them large documents, we must chop the text into smaller pieces, or 'chunks'. But how we chop it makes a massive difference!

cards.title: What is Fixed-Size Chunking?
cards.body: Fixed-size chunking is a method of splitting a large document into smaller pieces based solely on a set number of characters or tokens. It completely ignores natural formatting like paragraphs, sentences, or punctuation.

cards.title: The Sandwich Cutter
cards.body: Think of fixed-size chunking like cutting a long sandwich into exactly 3-inch pieces, regardless of where the cheese, tomatoes, or toppings actually lie. You might cut right through a slice of tomato, just like fixed-size chunking cuts right through a word or sentence boundary!

cards.title: A Fragmented Sentence
cards.body: If you set your chunk size to 10 characters, the sentence 'I love learning about AI' becomes 'I love lea', 'rning abou', and 't AI'. The meaning of the words is broken up because the algorithm only cares about the character count.

cards.title: The Smart Splitter Myth
cards.body: People often think that computers naturally understand where to split text for AI. In reality, a simple fixed-size splitter doesn't know what a sentence is; it just counts characters like a ruler measuring inches.

cards.title: Visualizing the Split
cards.body: Compare how fixed-size chunking behaves versus how our brains naturally expect text to be divided.

left.text: Splits exactly at 15 characters: 'I love building a|pps with AI.'

right.text: Splits at logical boundaries: 'I love building apps' | 'with AI.'

exercises.prompt: How does a fixed-size chunking algorithm decide where to cut a document?

options.text: It looks for periods and question marks to split the text.
It splits text at a strict character or token count, ignoring sentences.
It groups text by matching topics and themes.
It automatically translates the text before splitting it.

exercises.explain: Fixed-size chunking is 'blind' to semantics and syntax; it only counts out the exact number of characters or tokens specified and cuts the text there.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Fixed-size chunking is computationally cheap and easy to implement.
exercises.explain: Fixed-size chunking is highly predictable and fast because it doesn't perform complex language analysis; it simply counts units of text.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how fixed-size chunking measures its splits.
exercises.sentence: Fixed-size chunking splits a document by counting a set number of characters without looking at sentence boundaries.
exercises.explain: Fixed-size chunking splits text strictly by a predefined number of characters or tokens, paying no attention to sentence structures or meaning.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs shows text split by a naive fixed-size chunker set to exactly 13 characters (including spaces)?
exercises.explain: The first output shows a typical fixed-size chunk split right in the middle of words ('engi', 'neer'), which is a classic indicator of a simple, blind character-count split. The others respect word boundaries.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a document search system. Why might you choose fixed-size chunking, and what major downside must you prepare for?

options.text: It dynamically analyzes the semantic meaning of sentences to group them together.
It guarantees that complete sentences are never broken apart.
It is computationally simple and fast, but it often cuts sentences or words in half.
It requires an expensive LLM call to decide where to split the text document.

exercises.explain: Fixed-size chunking is incredibly fast and simple because it only counts characters or tokens. However, because it ignores natural language boundaries, it frequently splits key facts, names, or sentences in half, causing the LLM to lose the connection between related words.
